title: Magnetoelastic Waves in Ferromagnetic Thin Films Mediated by Dipolar Interactions
authors: Hiroki Yoshida, Ryohei Kono, Manato Fujimoto, Motoki Asano, Daiki Hatanaka, Kei Yamamoto, Shuichi Murakami
abstract: Magnetoelastic coupling mediated by magnetic dipolar interactions is theoretically investigated in ferromagnetic thin films under an in-plane magnetic field. We develop a theoretical description that incorporates dipolar fields derived from Maxwell's equations in the presence of elastic deformations. The resulting coupled equations of motion predict hybridization between magnetostatic and Lamb waves. Numerical calculations for a yttrium iron garnet (YIG) film reveal anti-crossings in the dispersion relations, with hybridization gaps ranging from $0.1$ to several MHz.
summary: This paper investigates how magnetic dipolar interactions contribute to the coupling between magnetic and elastic waves in ferromagnetic thin films. By incorporating elastic deformations into the derivation of dipolar fields, the authors show that magnetostatic waves and Lamb waves can hybridize. Using YIG as a model material, they demonstrate the appearance of frequency gaps at anti-crossings. The study also highlights how the symmetry of the applied magnetic field dictates the strength and presence of this coupling.
main_problem: The lack of a unified theoretical framework to describe magnetoelastic coupling in ferromagnetic thin films specifically mediated by long-range magnetic dipolar interactions.
main_result: The study predicts the hybridization of magnetostatic waves and Lamb waves in YIG thin films, identifying anti-crossings with hybridization gaps ranging from 0.1 to several MHz.
method: A Lagrangian-based approach incorporating Maxwell's equations and elastic deformations, solved using the Finite Element Method (FEM) and a Green-function approach.
model_or_system: An infinitely extended, isotropic ferromagnetic thin film (specifically Yttrium Iron Garnet) of thickness L under an in-plane external magnetic field.
key_observables: Dispersion relations (frequency vs. wavenumber), hybridization gaps, and anti-crossings.
important_parameters: Film thickness L = 500 nm, external field mu0H0 = 0.1 T, and YIG material parameters (density, Lamé constants, gyromagnetic ratio, and saturation magnetization).
main_assumptions: Magnetostatic approximation (neglecting retardation effects), linearized dynamics (small deviations of magnetization and displacement), and the quasi-static condition.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the system and the mechanism of deformation-induced dipolar modification; Figure 2 displays dispersion curves and anti-crossings for different magnetic field orientations (phi = 0, pi/4, pi/2).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dipolar-mediated coupling, develops a mathematical framework using Lagrangian and Eulerian coordinate transformations, derives the effective field and force density, and concludes with numerical FEM simulations of YIG films.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the system under consider- ation. A ferromagnetic thin film with thickness L in the z direction and infinite extent in the x and y directions is con- sidered. Magnetic moments precess around an axis parallel to the in-plane external magnetic field H0, which is applied at an angle φ with respect to the x axis. The elastic wave changes the positions of the magnetic moments and their sep- arations, resulting in a modification of the dipolar interaction among them.

FIG. 2. Dispersion curves of the coupled waves. Red and blue curves represent modes with C2x eigenvalues +1 and −1, respectively. Black curves represent magnetostatic modes that are not eigenstates of the C2x operator. Markers show the dispersion of the coupled waves obtained from numerical calculations. The external magnetic field is applied (a) parallel to the propagation direction, H0 ∥k (φ = 0), (b) at φ = π/4, and (c) perpendicular to the propagation direction, H0 ⊥k (φ = π/2). Insets show magnified views of representative band crossings. In (a), φ = 0, anticrossings appear between BVW and Lamb modes only when they have the same C2x eigenvalue (same color).
